questions: What is the correct step ordering for the CLI multi-token config changes?
How does loadConfig maintain backward compatibility while supporting multi-token config?
How should hlx token add handle the --url flag default?
How does hlx org switch resolve org names locally?
How do env-var overrides interact with the new multi-token config?

answers.answer: Step 1: Redesign config module (types, loadConfig, helpers). Step 2: Create hlx token add command. Step 3: Rewrite hlx org list (local-only). Step 4: Rewrite hlx org switch (local-only). Step 5: Update main entry point, usage, org dispatcher. Step 6: Quality gates. Step 1 is foundational — all other steps depend on the new config types and helpers. Steps 2-4 are independent of each other but all depend on Step 1. Step 5 wires everything together.
evidence: config.ts L12-19: current HxConfig type — Step 1 extends this
No token add command exists — Step 2 creates it
switch.ts resolves via server — Step 4 makes local-only
index.ts command dispatch — Step 5 adds token command

answers.answer: loadConfig() continues to return HxConfig | null. Internally it reads the orgs array, finds the entry matching currentOrg, and returns { apiKey: entry.token, url: entry.url, orgId: entry.orgId, orgName: entry.orgName }. All consumers (hxFetch, runInspect, runComments, etc.) see the same { apiKey, url, orgId, orgName } shape they always have. Zero changes needed in hxFetch or any command that consumes HxConfig. The env-var path (L26-29) returns { apiKey, url } immediately, bypassing the multi-token config entirely.
evidence: config.ts L24-49: loadConfig returns HxConfig | null
http.ts L53-61: hxFetch reads config.apiKey and config.orgId
config.ts L26-29: env-var path returns early

answers.answer: If --url not provided: use URL from first existing org entry (if any). If no entries exist and no --url, print error and exit. Rationale: most users have one server. Requiring --url on every hlx token add when adding a second token for the same server is poor UX. But the first token must establish the server URL. Each entry stores its own url, so tokens for different servers coexist.

Match input against local orgs array in priority order: (1) exact orgId match, (2) exact alias match, (3) case-insensitive orgName match. If no match: error with list of configured orgs. If multiple name matches: error listing all matches with IDs (fail on ambiguity). This replaces the prior server-based resolution via /api/auth/me availableOrganizations.
evidence: switch.ts L25-48: current server-based resolution to be replaced
tech-research/tech-research.md: hlx org switch resolution order confirmed

answers.answer: Completely unchanged. loadConfig (L26-29) checks env vars first: HELIX_API_KEY > HELIX_INSPECT_TOKEN > HELIX_INSPECT_API_KEY for token; HELIX_URL > HELIX_INSPECT_BASE_URL > HELIX_INSPECT_URL for URL. When env vars are set, loadConfig returns { apiKey, url } immediately (no orgId, no orgName). The multi-token orgs array is never consulted. This is correct — env vars override for Helix agent runs, which don't need local config.
evidence: config.ts L26-29: env var checks return early with { apiKey, url }
Continuation context: 'If env token variables are present, they must continue to override local config'

statement: The CLI implementation plan restructures config to support an orgs array with currentOrg pointer while maintaining HxConfig return type compatibility. Six steps: config module redesign with helper functions, new hlx token add command that validates via /api/auth/me, local-only hlx org list, local-only hlx org switch with ID/alias/name resolution, main entry point wiring, and quality gates. The env-var override path is completely unchanged. hxFetch and all existing commands work without modification through the compatibility layer.
